data.title: S&P 500: A Euphoric Market With A Sobering Ceasefire And GDP Reality
data.description:
The S&P 500 has gained 2.2% following the announcement of a ceasefire. While the reaction is not completely unexpected, I do believe that the optimism seen on the stock markets is premature as the ceasefire is fragile at best. In this report, I discuss the uncertainty that remains, which could eventually increase market volatility or in the worst case a continuation of the conflict.

Oil Prices Remain High And GDP Impact Is Still Present

There is no question that the worst has been averted in terms of oil prices, GDP impacts, and potential loss of lives. However, we note that the GDP impact from oil, LNG, and fertilizers was expected to be around 100 basis points on GDP with a 130 basis point global impact. That was for a situation where the war would last around 2-4 weeks with Brent oil at $100-$120, and those 2-4 weeks were measured from mid-March. The most optimistic scenario back then was the war ending immediately with Brent oil at $80-$88 per barrel and GDP impact expected to be 54 basis points. In this scenario inflation would be 0.7 points higher, and in the 2-4 week scenario inflation would be 1.4 points higher. So, while there is a ceasefire on the table now, there is a component of GDP and inflation that will affect growth. That seems to be irreversible. Oil is now trading at $94.85, which is substantially lower than the $115.69 peak, but it is still 44% higher than pre-war pricing. There have been 5 weeks in which prices have quickly risen, and that has an economic impact.

I believe that through elasticity, that could imply a 3%-12% downside for the S&P 500 measured from the pre-war trading levels. That would imply an S&P 500 at 6,050-6,675 points with a midpoint at around 6,365 points. The bottom was around 6,346 points, so pretty close to the midpoint of the range. At the time of writing, the S&P 500 is trading at 6,779, largely ignoring the GDP impact. So, the market is actually treating the GDP impact as a back-view mirror event, while the GDP impact still has to be absorbed. That disconnect is typical for the financial markets but may provide an indication that the market is not expecting or accurately mapping any GDP impact that has yet to come. In other words, we have seen a risk premium normalization in the markets but not yet a potential GDP impact being factored into the stock market.

The Ceasefire Is Fragile And Strait of Hormuz Remains Choked

I believe that the increase in the indices and the decline in oil prices is an optimistic one driven by optimism from President Trump. JD Vance, however, called the ceasefire a fragile one and if we look at the development, it indeed seems to be fragile. We have seen Gulf countries intercepting missiles after the ceasefire. It is a violation of the ceasefire. In some way it is also understandable. In Iran, with the elimination of Khamenei on day 1 of the strikes, there has been a power vacuum and it largely seems like the command has been decentralized meaning that it may take time before all regions and branches of command require time to adjust to the ceasefire. This is the consequence of a power vacuum, but by design this can also be created to inflict maximum damage when Iran’s leadership is no longer functional.

Furthermore, Israel may not view Lebanon as a part of the ceasefire, which may result in the ceasefire not holding. Several minutes after I wrote this sentence, the news came in that Iran signaled it may withdraw over continued attacks in Lebanon. We have also seen attacks on the Iranian oil refinery in Lavan. Whether the attack came from outside, was orchestrated by the regime, or came from inside is not known, but it feeds the view that the ceasefire is fragile and, in fact, more fragile than it seemed at the time of the announcement of the ceasefire. So far, it seems this is a truce between Iran and the US, and Israel has followed with a stop on attacks on Iran, but attacks in the region are still happening, so ceasefire means nothing more and nothing less than the US not attacking, but apart from that, the region is still in chaos.

The perception is that the Strait of Hormuz is now open for business, and the reality is that it is not. A toll that needs to be paid by shipping companies is seen as a potential joint venture by President Trump, but it actually means that Iran is gaining much needed funds on a ceasefire that may not remain in place. Iranian and Israeli new agencies are now reporting that the Strait of Hormuz is closed again. Opening the Strait of Hormuz seemed to be prerequisite for a ceasefire and the Strait now seems to be closed again less than a day after a ceasefire agreement was reached. Even if the Strait were to be fully open, it would take weeks, if not more, before supply would normalize, with some supply being lost due to damage to energy infrastructure.

Opposing Views On The 10-Point Plan

Pivotal for the ceasefire agreement seems to be a 10-point plan. It is reported that the plan includes Iran maintaining leverage of the Strait of Hormuz, removal of sanctions, removal of troops in neighboring countries, and continued nuclear enrichment. That 10-point list is a stark contrast with President Trump’s post on Truth Social and posted by the White House on X claiming no nuclear enrichment.

It seems that the U.S. 15-point plan and Iran’s 10-point plan have been mixed into a last-minute ceasefire to prevent a catastrophe. However, both parties seem to have entered lists that cannot be reconciled and that adds yet another fragile element to ceasefire.

Conclusion: Ceasefire Is A Great Thing, But This One Looks Shaky

Last night I could see four of the six KC-135 aerial tankers that are stationed at Bucharest departing when I was standing on my roof. Several hours later two tankers disappeared which often happens when fighter jets and B-52 bombers are being refueled. So, it seems we were hours away from something that could shape the global economy for years to come. In that regard, I believe the ceasefire is the best thing that could happen and I understand the positive market reaction. However, the current market reaction does not price in the fact that GDP still has been lost and oil and gas supply may take weeks to normalize, and the ceasefire is already being broken left and right, with the Strait of Hormuz seemingly being closed again. So, the conditions of the ceasefire and the ceasefire itself only exist on paper, while the market has already removed the war risk element from the stock market. I believe that the market reaction is overly enthusiastic and largely ignores economic realities as well as the reality of the fragile ceasefire. As a result, I believe that in a reasonable market setting the rally will not hold, and I believe that assigning a sell rating for now is prudent, and a more bullish view is justified based on what will be agreed on in the next two weeks.

data.title: Golar LNG: Structural LNG Tightness And Strategic Optionality Support Further Upside
data.description:
Here at the Lab, we were lucky on Golar LNG's (GLNG) initiation of coverage and follow-up note ahead of the Q3 2025 results. While we had not anticipated an escalation in the Middle East, our outlook was underpinned by supportive industry tailwinds. This was driven by structural growth stemming from heightened energy security concerns, with dynamics reinforced by Russia’s export restrictions (the EU gas diversification strategy) and the series of energy shocks in recent years. From a fundamentals view, our strong buy rating was backed by de-risked long-term contracts with a robust EBITDA backlog across multiple assets.

We report that, since mid-Oct 2025, the price of Golar LNG shares has increased by more than 40% (Fig. 1).

Fig 1

Why are we still positive?

Firstly, the company has initiated a formal process to evaluate strategic alternatives to maximize shareholders' value. Golar LNG appoints Goldman Sachs as a financial advisor. These strategic options include a company sale, asset divestment, and a merger or other business combination to optimize the company's corporate structure further. This was highlighted in our initiation of coverage, where we reported that "Golar investment may be viewed as a potential exit from an infrastructure fund targeting an unlevered annual return of 8-10%. Even if we are not speculating on M&A, this is a real case." A sale to an infra player is a potential outcome, but we believe Golar growth might also be of interest to strategic players in the oil and gas business.

Secondly, considering the Middle East escalation, we need to report Iran's attacks on the Ras Laffan in Qatar. This strike had removed 13 million tonnes of Qatar's LNG export capacity. Looking at the press release, they communicated that they will "declare force majeure on LNG contracts for up to five years." Therefore, it would take years to repair the damage.

There are two considerations for Golar:

  1. part of the company's upside is linked to gas price evolution, and this is positive for Hilli and MKIl, and
  2. this negative event is likely to raise Golar awareness and FLNG offering.

This is because the company produces floating liquefied natural gas vessels with a shorter time-to-market (3-4 years). Additionally, the company communicated cost inflation for equipment (especially gas turbines); however, Golar maintains a cost advantage over onshore alternatives of approximately 40%.

For the first time, the company reported "increasing demand for incremental FLNG project developments" (Fig. 2). So there is a valuation for additional FLNG units. Given that each unit generates more than $200 million in EBITDA per year, this could have significant upside for Golar's valuation.

Fig 2

Lastly, the company reported a Q4 adjusted EBITDA of $91 million, above the Wall Street estimate of $85.6 million. Higher top-line sales drove this beat, along with lower-than-expected G&A. Golar maintained its quarterly cash dividend and repurchased and canceled $43 million of common shares. More importantly, the company completed a refinancing for the Gimi facility for a total of $1.2 billion. We report positively on Golar's debt management activities, and, more importantly, Golar communicated that "we think that it's almost better to buy back our own stock at an undervaluation than to do anything else effectively." This is very supportive.

Valuation

It is very hard to increase our EBITDA estimates on higher gas price curves. In a supportive scenario, we see Golar EBITDA up by $45-$50 million per year. However, this isn't part of our playbook. We continue to value the company with a long-term contracted EBITDA of $850 million. So, we derive an enterprise value of $8.5 billion at a 10x EV/EBITDA multiple (unchanged from our last analysis). The company's debt totaled $2.7 billion, but after accounting for cash, the net debt was $1.5 billion. Compared to our last analysis, Golar slightly increased its net debt (we estimate it at $1.31 billion). This was due to the ongoing buyback. In our equity bridge, we also include $500 million in capex awarded for the required infrastructure for the FLNG Hilli and MKII FLNG projects. Therefore, total cash outflow is estimated at $2 billion, moving Golar's equity value to $6.5 billion. Considering a current market cap of $5.5 billion, there is an 18% upside, and with the current dividend payment, Golar's 1-year total return could reached 20%. So we remain buyers with a target price of $65 per share.

Risks

Golar risks include higher CAPEX, which could be detrimental to the equity value bridge. As reported in our analysis, we see CAPEX inflation risks, especially for turbines. Golar is subject to clients' concentration and operates in EM; however, the company operates under English Law, with dispute resolution pursuant to International Chamber of Commerce arbitration in France. The company may be exposed to technological changes, and, given its current debt, Golar is sensitive to higher interest rates.

Conclusion

All in all, we see upside ahead in the Golar investment equity story. We positively report the Goldman Sachs appointment for strategic optionality to maximize value. Additionally, the ongoing conflicts and energy diversification require an infrastructure player such as Golar.

data.title: Enovix: Simply Not Good Enough
data.description:
The bear case for Enovix (ENVX) is simple: the company has not gotten it done. The optimism around the company's 2021 SPAC (special purpose acquisition company) merger faded when the company's production equipment basically failed to perform as hoped. ENVX stock plunged 41% after the Q3 2022 earnings release when that problem was disclosed.

The arrival of T.J. Rodgers as executive chairman just days later sparked turnaround hopes, given Rodgers's success with Cypress Semiconductor and then Enphase Energy (ENPH). Rodgers quickly installed a new chief executive officer, Raj Talluri, with the clear goal of improving execution and getting Enovix's innovative silicon-anode batteries into production.

That hasn't worked, either. Deadlines continue to slip, while Talluri has (by his own admission at a fireside chat in December) overpromised and underdelivered. Rodgers has made, shall we say, unsubstantiated allegations against short sellers, while Enovix's capital allocation (alongside Rodgers's own comments) has seemed designed at least in part to play up the "short squeeze" angle and boost the stock. Here, too, the strategy hasn't succeeded: ENVX hit an all-time low last month and short interest remains significantly elevated.

The bull case, meanwhile, is that these short-term failures are simply delays, rather than a sign that the long-term case for Enovix stock is failing. To be sure, Enovix is undertaking an exceptionally difficult effort. Silicon-anode batteries have never been commercialized. And the opportunity obviously is massive: Enovix originally targeted the smartphone market, but management (particularly of late) has pointed to additional opportunities in smart eyewear, drones, and defense. If the company can finally develop a battery that offers (as management has detailed), longer life, better performance, and faster charging, the potential market is enormous, particularly with generative artificial intelligence driving ever-greater demands on battery-powered devices.

The problem, however, is that 'if' is just too big. With yet another disappointment delivered in Q4, it's become nearly impossible to have much faith in the company. And even if Enovix can drive a turnaround, the potential upside here remains significant enough that it seems wiser to buy after the company shows some progress rather than before. Without any progress — and Q4 showed little to none — Enovix's history still casts too large a shadow over its future.

The Honor Problem

In September 2024, Enovix entered into a development agreement with Chinese smartphone manufacturer Honor. Over the following months, the two companies worked together to qualify Enovix batteries for Honor products.

On the Q3 call, Talluri noted that while Enovix had come close to meeting Honor's requirements, it hadn't quite gotten there. The core stumbling point seemed to be over a 1,000 charge/discharge cycle test; as Talluri put it on the call, Enovix products could get close to that number, but near the end of the test performance would start to slip. And so Enovix agreed to a "design iteration", apparently focused on the actual chemistry of the batteries, which in turn would meet Honor's requirements. As Talluri put it in the Q&A of the call:

We realized that we need to make one more small design change to get to the full performance that they want. And we validated that change now internally, and we have confidence that, that change we made will actually go to the 1,000 cycles requirement.

We're now making batteries to that specification, and we expect to get those batteries and send them out to Honor this quarter, and they will start the testing again. And as you know, batteries are one of those things where when you give a new design iteration, we got to start the cycle life testing again, and it takes 3 to 4 months to do it because of just the nature of the process. We expect that to get done in 1Q next year, and then we expect to get the commercialization after that.

That's not what happened, however. As of the Q1 call in late February, Honor still hadn't qualified the product, and in fact Enovix seems to have made basically zero progress in the nearly four months between earnings releases. The answer from Talluri now in fact seems to be that Enovix can't meet Honor's existing requirements, given the three scenarios to qualification he laid out after Q1:

  1. Honor basically accepts testing under different conditions, notably around how much of the battery is discharged (0.2C versus 0.7C, or discharging the battery fully in five hours instead of 1.4 hours);
  2. A new "accelerated testing protocol" suitable for silicon-anode batteries. Talluri has argued (likely with some justification) that current testing structure doesn't reflect real-world usage and that the new type of battery perhaps merit a new test;
  3. Continued chemistry changes to meet the existing 0.7C test that Enovix hasn't been able to completely pass to this point.

The fact that a) the 0.7C test apparently has been a stumbling block for potentially a year (ie, a few months after Honor came on as lead customer ~18 months ago) and b) Talluri is highlighting the other two options first both suggest the third option is unlikely to arrive soon, if at all. (If the CEO was confident that chemistry changes would let batteries pass the 0.7C test, he wouldn't have spent months trying to get Honor to junk that very same test.)

But the very fact that Honor hasn't agreed to either of the other two scenarios itself is a problem (and one that grows bigger by the day). These are essentially binary decisions from Honor: either the smartphone manufacturer accepts Enovix's arguments about altering testing specifications or it doesn't. This doesn't seem like a negotiable point, particularly because Enovix has no leverage. It can't threaten to withhold a product that doesn't actually yet exist (at least in commercialized form). Meanwhile, Honor would be taking a very real risk (from its perspective) in using Enovix batteries: shipping a few million phones that were potentially found to have substandard batteries would create massive costs and damage to the brand.

And so investors have to at least contemplate the potential that the partnership here simply is going to end. If Enovix can't get over the finish line at some point Honor is going to move on. That point is absolutely destructive for the bull case here. Enovix ostensibly chose Honor as lead customer in the first place because it was the manufacturer most willing to work with it and consider a form of battery that, again, has never been commercialized before. If Honor walks away, it's exceptionally difficult to see what options Enovix has. There aren't that many smartphone manufacturers, Enovix has had years to attempt to reach out to them, and if any others were more enthused than Honor in 2024 they would themselves be the lead customer(s).

So the issues with Honor are not just a setback, or a delay. They legitimately appear to threaten the bull case for ENVX stock that has existed over the nearly five years the company has been public.

Can Enovix Even Commercialize Its Batteries?

Again, it's possible that analysis is too pessimistic, and that Enovix does find a way to reach qualification with Honor in the not-too-distant future. No doubt, that would drive a huge upside move for ENVX stock; from a short-term perspective, in fact, the stock is likely a bet on whether Honor qualification happens or not. (From here, that's a reason to stay on the sidelines at the least, but some readers admittedly may view it differently.)

But taking the longer-view, the last few years and quarters raise concerns about an issue that doesn't get as much coverage, which is whether Enovix can actually make any money off these batteries. Qualification at a single customer might drive a big pop in the stock, but for Enovix as a business it's only one step, and an early step at that.

At the very least, Enovix hasn't proven that it can do so. On the Q4 call, the issue of laser dicing in the manufacturing process came up again as a potential "limiting factor" in throughput. Yields are nearing 80% overall, but the dicing problems are driving lower performance (though, to be fair, Talluri said the performance has improved in the first two months of this year).

Overall, Talluri continues to argue that any yield and throughput concerns will be resolved — but that in and of itself highlights a core problem with the bull case, which is that it's at this point impossible to take that at face value. Talluri was saying months after taking over that full-scale manufacturing would begin in late 2024 and ramp across 2025. Commercialization per the Q4 call is now "late '26 or beginning in 2027", and even that's far from guaranteed.

But importantly, that's not the final step, or anywhere close. In fact, it's actually the less difficult step — and Enovix hasn't even started taking it yet. It's been commercialization and yields where battery upstarts have often stumbled, and Enovix hasn't proved it can tackle either problem yet.

No, ENVX Stock Is Not Cheap

One retort to these issues is that Enovix actually can still prosper with a small (or perhaps even nonexistent) smartphone business, because other attractive end markets now are available. Talluri made this case after Q4, and so did the company's earnings presentation:

And there is some logic to Talluri's point, which is that smart eyewear and drones are actually easier markets to get qualified in. Drones, for instance, have plenty of space if a battery needs to expand (the core issue for silicon-anode, and one which Enovix plans to solve through the structure of the casing as much as the interior chemistry). Smart eyewear customer have lower bars to clear for qualification; the discussion around the back-and-forth with Honor does suggest that Enovix might have little issue getting qualified for eyewear.

But even though the slide above suggests relatively large addressable markets, it's not at all clear that they are big enough to support the current ENVX stock price. The company still has an enterprise value of over $1 billion. Margins in the industry are lower, with gross margins often in the teens and operating margins for most consumer players in the low to mid-single-digits:

Ostensibly, Enovix would garner higher margins with a product that worked in the way management believes it will. Even so, a $1B-plus EV still prices in at least a couple billion dollars in annual revenue at some point several years out. (Just back of the envelope, something like $2 billion in 2032 sales at a 10% operating margin probably supports the current EV, given there is obviously a very real risk of a zero here if the technology simply can't be produced at scale.)

But per the call, the total addressable market for smart eyewear is supposed to be $400 million by 2030. Per the slide above, defense and drones is ~$3 billion now; that's maybe $5 billion down the line (right now, drones are about half the figure).

In other words, in a scenario where smartphone revenue doesn't materialize, Enovix stock still is pricing in not just a couple wins in eyewear and drones, but material market share in a hugely competitive market. That's with minimal business development efforts or infrastructure in those industries at the moment. The risks there in practice strongly suggest that "Enovix can just pivot away from smartphones" is not a solid enough bull case to own shares here.

So even at the lows, Enovix stock still is pricing in some success here. And to return to the point made at the beginning of this article, from a high level to this point Enovix basically has had zero success. (The Routejade acquisition admittedly does seem like a smart move, but in the context of valuation it's just not big enough or profitable enough to materially change the investment case.)

And even in a scenario where Enovix does find a way to commercialize smartphone batteries, there is enough upside that trying to time the win doesn't seem wise from a risk/reward standpoint. ENVX might well go to $20-plus on the back of a legitimate, consistent, profitable smartphone business. (That hypothetical valuation might well be conservative; the stock traded at $20-plus quite often over the past few years, suggesting a path toward much higher levels if the blue-sky scenario played out.). Owning it at $9 with some evidence of success and a clearer path toward billions in revenue seems wiser from a risk/reward standpoint than trying to time the bottom at $5.

After all, the bull case here is triple-digit upside, and the bear case is zero. It would seem to make more sense betting on the bull case when and if Enovix finally provides a concrete reason to do so.

data.title: SCHP: Schwab TIPS Fund Provides A Good Medium Duration Alternative To VTIP
data.description:
TIPS are Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities issued by the US Government. This type of security is described further in the Summary Prospectus of Schwab U.S. TIPS ETF (SCHP), which is also the fund that we will be covering today.

TIPS are publicly issued, dollar denominated U.S. Government securities issued by the U.S. Treasury that have principal and interest payments linked to an official inflation measure (as measured by the Consumer Price Index, or CPI) and their payments are supported by the full faith and credit of the United States.

Source: Summary Prospectus

SCHP is a passive exchange-traded fund that pursues the performance of the Bloomberg US Treasury Inflation-Linked Bond Index (Series-L). The index does not have any expenses; however, the fund does.

The expenses are nominal but should still be taken into account when comparing the performance of the ETF versus its benchmark index. We can see that SCHP has comfortably achieved its investment objective, which is "to track as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the total return of an index composed of inflation-protected U.S. Treasury securities."

The fund also sports 4 stars (out of 5) for overall performance from Morningstar.

We look at whether this would make a good holding for your portfolio today.

Why TIPS?

With nominal yield bonds, you are locking in the duration risk and the inflation risk. If inflation turns out to be higher than expected over the course of the bond term, your real returns will be lower. TIPS are a fix for the second problem. With TIPS, you only lock in a "real" return over the duration of the bond. This means you are taking some duration risk (depending on the length of the bond), but you are not messing with inflation risk. If inflation (as measured by the Consumer Price Index, or CPI) soars over the period, well, you will make a lot more dough. Perhaps the best visualization of this can be done via the post-COVID-19 period. The government had unleashed the largest fiscal stimulus of all time, and the Fed had also stepped up to the stupidity by buying inflated mortgage-backed securities. Inflation soared. You can see how SCHP outperformed a standard iShares 7-10 Treasury Bond ETF (IEF) during this time.

Yes, it still had negative returns, but that positive differential comes entirely from the surprise in inflation.

One reason that SCHP did not do even better is because the real rates soared during that timeframe. Investors who were happy to purchase bonds at negative real rates (guaranteed loss after inflation), simply because they read somewhere that bond duration should match their expected life span, found out the hard way.

The gains from extra inflation payments were offset partially by this soaring real rates effect on the portfolio. That brings us to the key attraction today. The real rate, as shown above, while not extremely high, is at least decent. You are locking in a modest real rate, and that means your odds of making money over 5 years are extremely high.

Verdict on SCHP

While we have not covered SCHP prior to this, we have written on Vanguard Short-Term Inflation-Protected Sec Index ETF (VTIP) several times. Even during our maximum bearish phase on bonds, we found this one appealing. The key difference between VTIP and SCHP comes down to duration. VTIP has a very short effective duration of 2.5 years, and that makes it virtually risk-free as an investment. You are taking no credit risk, and you are taking no inflation risk. What you are taking with VTIP is just mild duration risk. In fact, during the post-COVID-19 blowout in bonds, VTIP actually managed to even clobber SCHP.

But today, we think that SCHP's extra duration is not a bad idea. About half of its portfolio has a duration that averages similar to VTIP (the 1-3 years and 3-5 years categories). The rest is the extra duration that we are talking about.

You want to lock in that real rate, at least on a portion of your investments, and we think SCHP does a reasonably good job at that. While bonds remain hated, we think this is a good time to add some inflation-protected bonds to the mix. The main risk (we can't use "real risk" here) is that real rates soar. We don't believe that outcome will be allowed, as that would rapidly lead to a US Treasury default. But in all normal circumstances, these bonds should provide a good diversification for your portfolio. While we do not own these, we do own some real return bonds in Canada, which function on the same principle.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Trade Desk: Macro Headwinds Aside, This Is A Tremendous Bargain (Upgrade)
data.description:
There's no doubt that 2026 has introduced substantial volatility into both the stock market and the "real economy." Overall, companies are watching a trend of potentially weaker consumer spending, which has resulted in many brands pulling back on advertising spend.

The weaker advertising climate, plus a rising narrative that Amazon (AMZN) and AI are growing threats, has doused cold water on The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD), formerly a market darling that has now lost ~85% of its peak value and is down ~50% since the start of the year alone. The question for investors now is, when does the bleeding stop?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on The Trade Desk in December, when the stock was trading at $39 per share. Needless to say, I didn't predict the SaaSpocalypse or for continued pessimism around The Trade Desk to tank the share price, and my earlier "Buy" call was ill-timed. That said, when I take a fresh look at the company's fundamentals now (including and especially its decimated valuation multiples), I find a deeply oversold stock that may require patience but is ripe for a turnaround. I'm upgrading my viewpoint on The Trade Desk to a "Strong Buy."

Deep Valuation Discount Ahead Of Solid Guidance For FY26

A "Strong Buy" rating represents a high level of conviction that I have in very few stocks, and in The Trade Desk's case, my high degree of conviction lies primarily in the fact that there is a huge valuation safety net underneath this stock. Herd mentality has intensified in the stock market of 2026, which has driven many investors to flee to safety, especially after "SaaSpocalypse" fears. But we shouldn't ignore the stocks that have crashed and offer deep value.

At current share prices under $20, The Trade Desk trades at a market cap of $9.57 billion. We note the company's latest balance sheet has $1.30 billion of cash, completely unencumbered by debt. Adjusting for this gives us an enterprise value of $8.27 billion.

The Trade Desk only guides out one quarter at a time, but we can utilize the company's latest expectations to construct a viewpoint on the company's potential for profitability this year. For Q1, the company has guided to $678 million in revenue (+10% y/y) and $195 million in adjusted EBITDA, or a 29% margin that represents ~5 points of downside versus a 34% margin in FY25.

Is the slowdown to ~10% growth, plus the expectation of a pullback in adjusted EBITDA margins, concerning? Absolutely: We won't completely ignore the fact that The Trade Desk sits in a far more competitive advertising landscape, and the fact that the company is expecting margins to flatten is a signal of the intensifying competitive landscape that investors have been fearing. But at the same time, these risks are priced in.

For the full year FY26, Wall Street is expecting The Trade Desk to generate $3.26 billion in revenue, or 13% y/y growth. If we assume that full-year margins follow the same seasonality as in FY25 and decline 5 points (as suggested by the company's Q1 guidance) to a 36% full-year margin (versus 41% for the full year FY25), then FY26 adjusted EBITDA would be $1.17 billion (-2% y/y). Also, Street estimates are pointing to $2.06 in pro forma EPS (+16% y/y), driven in large part by declining share counts.

This positions The Trade Desk's valuation at:

  • 7.1x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA.
  • 9.7x FY26 P/E.
  • 8.4x FY26 ex-cash P/E.

Again, we emphasize that the low multiples help to compensate for a riskier operating landscape. At the same time, the market is treating The Trade Desk like a company that is at near-term risk of implosion, when in reality, it's still a business that is generating strong earnings. We suspect that these multiples, at the very least, attract potential acquirers (whether PE firms looking for healthy cash flow in a completely unlevered company or a potential strategic acquirer like Alphabet (GOOG), who could combine The Trade Desk's automated ad-buying software with its reams of digital inventory).

Q4 Wasn't As Bad As Feared

The other point to make is that, despite the negativity that has built up in this stock, its actual fundamental reality and execution aren't disastrous. Take a look at the Q4 earnings summary below:

The Trade Desk's revenue grew 14% y/y to $846.8 million, which actually still beat Wall Street's expectations of $840.7 million (+13% y/y) by a one-point margin. Recall as well that the company had a tough prior-year comp against the 2024 election cycle last year. Management notes that excluding revenue tied to political advertising, Q4 growth would have clocked in at 19% y/y (decelerating three points from 22% growth in Q3, adjusted for political advertising in that quarter as well).

There are two main concerns that investors have about The Trade Desk right now: its sales performance, which is being colored by the macroeconomy, and the rise of Amazon's DSP as a threat to The Trade Desk. Though we certainly don't favor The Trade Desk's revenue deceleration (especially to ~10% growth in Q1), we also have to keep the macro in mind. Per CEO Jeffrey Green's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

2025 was fantastic for tech spend, for travel spend, for pharma spend and for communication spend. Actually, despite a greater degree of macro uncertainty and most S&P 500 companies trying to determine what changes the evolving AI-fueled world and the geopolitical issues mean for them, most categories had a very good year. One of the clearest themes in our data and from our conversations with clients was a sustained weakness among some large consumer packaged goods companies, CPGs as well as some global auto companies.

Together, these verticals represent over 1/4 of our business. But in these 2 categories, all global companies have levels of uncertainty that we haven't seen for most of the last 15 years. They all had tough choices to make in 2025. Most still have tough choices ahead. I do want to be clear that some autos and CPGs have done very well in this environment, especially those that have focused on growth, value, objectivity and impact.

But some have had to respond to the impact of those macro pressures and instead just focus on reducing costs. In other cases, some companies have shrunk branding spend and focused on cost cutting instead of focusing on growing. When these companies are excluded from our year-over-year comparison, our business in the open internet is doing much better than the averages alone would suggest.

We think these are transitory macro headwinds that we shouldn't overly punish The Trade Desk for. The U.S. economy is shaky but more robust than many thought (as evidenced by the strong recent jobs report). The Trade Desk should benefit once businesses pivot back to growth mode, which may happen upon greater clarity on the end of the Iran conflict and an accommodating Fed.

The other issue is Amazon. Yes, it's true that Amazon has made growth in advertising revenue a major priority. At the same time, The Trade Desk's position is that Amazon is primarily concerned with selling inventory on Amazon (on Prime Video and in the marketplace). Yes, ad buyers on Amazon have better access to Amazon's retail data, and Amazon has tremendous pricing power to cut its platform fees (potentially to zero).

But businesses typically want to advertise across a wide variety of places in what The Trade Desk calls "the open internet," and that's where The Trade Desk fills buyer demand that can't be handled by Amazon. With The Trade Desk still projecting to grow double digits in FY26 despite a weak macro, we find that it's difficult to make the argument that Amazon is totally killing off The Trade Desk.

In Q4, The Trade Desk grew adjusted EBITDA 14% y/y to $400.3 million, at a 47% margin that held flat y/y. Full-year adjusted EBITDA of $1.20 billion also grew 18% y/y, with margins holding flat at 41%.

The company's Q1 guidance, which calls for margins eroding 5 points y/y, is the first time the company will have seen margins decline. This may be a conservative outlook that The Trade Desk may end up overcoming.

Risks And Key Takeaways

The next major catalyst for The Trade Desk will be its Q1 earnings report, due in mid/late May. Of course, there are risks here that we need to keep in mind. The macro situation likely worsened considerably relative to when the company issued its Q4 earnings (in February), thanks to the run-up in oil prices. It may take time for The Trade Desk to benefit from the macro stabilization that will help to resuscitate its revenue.

In my view, however, the stock's single-digit P/E and EBITDA multiples more than adequately price in macro and Amazon competitive risks. Use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Playtika Needs To Rescue Itself From An Acquisition That Turned Out Too Great
data.description:
Rescue itself? Too great?

How’s that for a counterintuitive combination of words in a headline!

But I’m completely serious.

And PLTK seems to also be on this same page. On 4/6/26, it announced that it’s exploring “strategic alternatives.” That usually means it’s looking for somebody to acquire it.

Nice.

The company announced a big loss and that it suspended its cash dividend during the 2/26/26 earnings call. So now, disappointed shareholders can bank on an M&A rescue.

I, too, am hoping for better times. I wrote up PLTK on 4/13/25 and said “Buy.” That aged dismally. The stock, since then, is down about 40%. The S&P 500 is up about 32%.

I had high hopes for PLTK’s SuperPlay acquisition.

Wait a minute…

The purchase I supported turned out better than expected. Yet the stock is in the dumpster and I penned a headline that uses the word “rescue.”

Obviously, there’s a lot to unpack here. So let’s get to it…

A (Very) Quick Introduction To PLTK

This company makes video games.

These are simple low-stakes games. They don’t require players to commit lots of time to develop skills, master complex strategies, etc.

They’re designed to be played on mobile devices.

And they’re free to download. Game proprietors like PLTK make money by selling ads within the games and/or through in-app purchases. With the latter, users pay small fees to advance to higher levels.

In the past, PLTK often expanded its game portfolio by making acquisitions.

And that leads us to the big one — the SuperPlay acquisition completed on 11/20/24. Besides adding two big gaming hits (Dice Dreams and Domino Dreams) to the PLTK portfolio, the purchase announcement said the deal also…

adds a talented development team known for delivering successful games. Together, Playtika and SuperPlay are well-positioned to deliver exceptional gaming experiences to players worldwide.

Not every acquirer correctly judges the talents of people who come aboard through an acquisition. But PLTK had reason to know. Two of SuperPlay’s three founders used to work for PLTK before leaving to launch SuperPlay in 2019. The third worked for Rovio, which is famous for the Angry Birds games.

And when it comes to the quality of what PLTK brought in, the company got it right. And strategically, it makes sense…

Reorienting The PLTK Portfolio

The are many ways to categorize mobile games. You can find sample taxonomies here and here.

I’m not inclined to favor any particular schema over any other. I’d rather than just keep things simple and relevant insofar as they apply to PLTK.

Social Casino - Mature

PLTK has long been a big-time entrant in a category known as social casino. These feature familiar gambling games like slots, blackjack, poker and bingo.

But you bet and win virtual coins, not real money. Winnings stay in the game as benefits that can be exchanged for advanced game features, or even bragging rights. Speaking of which…

Numerous studies show that people choose online social casino games because they help players connect with others, not only for entertainment. Survey-based research indicates that 29% of participants said they played for social reasons. Platforms reflect this with features like open chats, clubs, gifting, and leaderboards.

It’s a good category. But for PLTK, a big gorilla in the field, it’s mature, beset by competition, and now slow-growing.

Management hasn’t, as far as I’ve seen, expressly used the phrase “cash cow.” But from the way PLTK discusses its social casino games, it sounds to me like that’s what it is.

That’s OK. PLTK wouldn’t be the first business to prosper by investing money generated by solid cash cows into faster growing businesses. And that is, indeed, the goal here.

Casual - Growing Briskly

The company is instead shifting its focus to the better growing casual games category. This was a big motivation for the SuerPlay purchase. The deal brought huge casual hits to PLTK. These are Dice Dreams and Domino Dreams. And the first SuperPlay debut under PLTK ownership was Disney Solitaire. This, too, is shaping up as a major success.

PLTK exited the 2025 fourth quarter with about 74% of revenue coming from this category.

On the 2/28/26 earnings call, CEO Robert Antokol characterized SuperPlay as…

one of the fastest-growing studios in the mobile gaming industry at their scale. We acquired SuperPlay to add top casual games, bring a new growth engine and widen our base with long-life assets. The performance support this decision and raises our confidence in SuperPlay.

Later during the call, Antokol suggested there’s more to come…

[W]e have expanded our collaboration with Disney and Pixar Games and are developing a new title in the SuperPlay pipeline.

Direct to Consumer

In my 4/13/25 writeup, I discussed PLTK’s emerging Direct-to-Consumer (DTC) strategy. This is where players access games not through an app store but directly from a PLTK web site. With respect to this, I wrote…

DTC gives PLTK some strong advantages.

It helps the company foster player engagement and loyalty… PLTK can collect more detailed data and more effectively customize rewards, promotions and IAP bundles based on what it knows about its customers

It’s also good for the bottom line. PLTK need not pay the 30% commissions on IAPs demanded by the App Stores.

With DTC, it need not worry about what changing App-Store privacy dictates might impact its data-collection ability.

So far, it looks like this strategy is working well. Antokol said, on the earnings call, that…

D2C keeps growing and adds more value for Playtika. In Q4, D2C was 36.8% of our revenues, and we ended the year at about $1 billion in annualized D2C revenue.

But, Let’s Remember…

My headline used the word “rescue.”

And the stock’s miserable performance suggests I did not use that word in vain. So let’s step away from the good stuff.

It’s time to focus on…

Earnouts — PLTK’s Dark Cloud

This is an aspect of M&A. It’s setup in which…

the purchase price is contingent on the “future performance” of the target company. The acquirer pays a majority of the purchase price upfront, at the time of closing the deal, and the remainder is contingent on the performance of the target.

It’s used to get deals done even when buyers and sellers can’t agree on the value of the company being sold. The Seller is protected against having sold too cheaply. And the buyer is protected from overpaying.

SuperPlay exceeded the targets established in the acquisition agreement. That means PLTK has to pay more than the originally agreed-to price.

Generally, that’s a good problem for companies in PLTK’s position. According to a Wharton faculty blog…

multiple studies have suggested that 70-90% of deals underperform expectations.

PLTK’s SuperPlay purchase turned out to have been among the fortunate 10-30%. SuperPlay outperformed expectations.

Good for PLTK! Or maybe not…

Because the deal included earnout provisions, PLTK will have to pay more than the agreed-to price. And earnouts that get recorded on the acquirer’s books are periodically adjusted as more information surfaces about the target’s ongoing performance.

OK, you may think. SuperPlay is bringing in more money than initially expected. So why cry about paying up for the benefit it truly received.

In theory, that’s fine. But as it turns out, SuperPlay didn’t just meet or exceed original expectations. It blew way past them.

This is why the quarterly EPS table below is going to show, for the 2025 fourth quarter, a monstrous loss that appears to have come out from nowhere.

The original SuperPlay purchase price was $700 million. The earnout could add up to $1.25 billion depending on specified revenue and adjusted EBITDA targets for calendar years 2025, 2026 and 2027.

Accounting-wise, the earnout doesn’t impact cash flow. It’s recorded on the income statement as (a big) part of SG&A. But on the cash flow statement, it shows up as an addition to cash from operations (“Change in contingent consideration”).

But don’t think for a nano-second that PLTK will actually avoid parting with more cash later on. It’ll have to come from cash on hand and/or external capital. So GAAP rules aside, more money will exit from PLTK.

(If you really want to go deep into the weeds on this, you can do so starting on page 155 of the PDF version of the 2025 10K as linked to from Seeking Alpha.)

For today’s purposes, I think it’ll be enough to say that PLTK suspended (management’s word — you and I might instead say eliminated) its formerly nice cash dividend. And we’ll have to expect some increasing balance sheet challenges going forward as PLTK pays the earnouts.

Potential Rescue

As noted above, PLTK announced on 4/6/26, in its words, that it’s “exploring strategic alternatives.” In my words, and probably in the words of many in the investment community, it’s looking for a friendly acquirer or merger partner.

That makes sense. As we’re seeing and have seen in many parts of media and entertainment, scale is a good thing. M&A in this area has been going on seemingly forever. The recent Paramount Skydance (PSKY)-Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD)-Netflix (NFLX) battle was by no means a novel event.

Mobile gaming, PLTK’s bailiwick, is fertile ground for this sort of thing. It’s a crowded field. So, it takes more and more for games to attract and retain players.

Privately-owned Epic Games, which laid off more than 1,000 people due to declining user engagement with long-time mega-hit game Fortnite, shows us how no game company can ever take player retention for granted.

And my extremely contrarian 3/10/26 writeup of AppLoving (APP) discussed how excesses in in-game advertising, a favorite player acquisition strategy, is likely to come under increasing pressure and, I believe, lose effectiveness.

So, I see PLTK as another company that could benefit from more scale in order to better meet future game development, player retention, and player acquisition spending.

Now, we have to understand that PLTK made a prior announcement relating to a search for strategic alternatives back in 2022. That happened after the company unsuccessfully tried to buy Rovio.

It didn’t find an acquirer back then. But it did find a buyer for 25.73% of the PLTK stake owned that had been owned by PLTK’s China-based majority shareholder.

This time around, however, PLTK shapes up as a better buyout candidate or merger partner. It’s game portfolio, helped by SuperPlay, is stronger. And the proficiency it’s developing in Direct-to-Consumer seems like it could be very appealing.

PLTK shares jumped nearly 17% on April 6th, the day PLTK issues its strategic-alternatives announcement.

PLTK’s Numbers

Don’t expect this section to look pretty. As noted, there’s a gaming portfolio shift in progress. And marketing expenses are elevated. There’s also the earnouts.

So let’s run quickly through the numbers that we know won’t spark high hopes.

Here are the quarterly Revenue and EPS trends.

If PLTK is still reporting as an independent entity this time next year, ignore the 4Q26 estimate. Instead, expect earnouts to produce another big loss. And look for more of the same in 4Q27 assuming, as we should hope, the SuperPlay games continue to do well.

Here are the historic and future growth rates.

(I compare PLTK to medians among companies in the Interactive Home Entertainment industry and in the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Because PLTK is small, I also compare it to the medians of companies in the portfolio of the SPDR Portfolio S&P 600 Small Cap ETF (SPSM). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.)

Are you excited? I’m not. I could be if SuperPlay’s performance were less spectacular and I could look for better results (with lesser earnouts) going forward. But too-much-of-a-good-thing should keep earnouts high.

Here are the core fundamentals.

It’s the same story. I could live with this not-great set of numbers if I could expect improvement down the road. But here again, too-good is the cloud over future expectations.

Here are the financial statement highlights.

It’s the same story. It’s not great now. And the earnouts suggest a weaker balance sheet down the road. And, of course, we know the dividend is going to zero.

Risk

The risk is that the current quest for a buyer or some comparable rescue comes to naught. The stock market would truly hate that!

What to do About PLTK Stock

Here are the valuation ratios.

Usually, when there is no projected 3- to 5-year earnings grown rate in the database, I try to estimate or infer something for it and for a PEG FWD. Given the situation here, it’s not worthwhile.

Suffice it to say now that the PE FWD and the EV/S are so low as to be giving PLTK no credit at all for any potential growth in the future.

I get that the earnouts are a problem now. But suggesting no future growth at all seems to me like massive overkill.

The earnouts won’t last forever. They end after 2027. And PLTK still has a great games portfolio that combines cash cows with hot games and further efforts on the part of a skilled development team.

The PEG FWD would be a mere 1.00 if PLTK can generate even a 4.27% 3- to 5-year EPS growth rate.

Let’s not let pessimism get extreme. That 4.27% growth target should be more-than do-able.

Here’s the price chart.

That’s been terrible for much of the time. But Mr. Market is buying into a rescue scenario.

The 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) looks like it could soon cross above the 50-day EMA.

Consider, too, Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Both have recently become bullish as the Street looks ahead toward rescue, one way or another.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

I get that my original “Buy” rating turned out W-R-O-N-G. There’s no point in trying to soft-pedal that. But having messed up once before doesn’t mean I should mess up again.

I have to steel myself for the task at hand, to forget the unchangeable past and look to the future. When I do that, I see a stock that’s exceptionally depressed, even when we consider known challenges.

If I were seeing PLTK today for the first time ever, I’d say it’s a value play, perhaps even a cigar-butt value play. On that basis…

I see PLTK as likely outperforming the broader market.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by reiterating my prior, albeit badly aged, “Buy” rating.

data.title: Celestica: Mr. Market Gives Us A Perfect Opportunity To Load Up (Upgrade)
data.description:
Performance assessment

Celestica Inc. (CLS, CLS:CA) has once again outperformed the market since my last Buy update on the stock:

Elevator pitch

I am getting even more bullish on Celestica:

  1. Celestica is building capacity to facilitate long term demand
  2. HPS product mix shifts can structurally lift margins
  3. High customer concentration is still a risk to monitor
  4. Valuations seem attractive for long term investors
  5. Technicals show an A+ bull-flag continuation setup.

This article will use some Celestica-specific acronyms. If you are unfamiliar with Celestica, please refer to my initiating coverage article on Celestica to understand what CCS, ATS, HPS, and other things like networking switches mean.

Celestica is building capacity to address long term demand

Alphabet (GOOGL, GOOG) is one of Celestica's top customers. And this company recently announced a $180B AI capex plan for 2026, up 97% from FY25 levels of $91B. Celestica is well positioned to benefit from this spending as one of the key assemblers of Google's TPUs:

On this note, we are proud of our decade-long partnership with Google and are excited to continue supporting the acceleration of leading AI data center architecture. Celestica remains closely aligned with Google on the development of complex data center hardware and systems. As a preferred manufacturing partner for Google's Tensor processing unit, or TPU systems, Celestica is committed to making long-term investments in both capacity and capabilities both in the United States and across our global footprint, which includes our planned investments to expand manufacturing capacity in 2026 and 2027.

- CEO Robert Mionis in the Q4 FY25 earnings call.

This gives Celestica a flow of long-term demand that it is aiming to maximize by expanding capacity in a very big way, ramping up capex spend for FY26 to $1B from $200M in FY25:

... we are meaningfully increasing the scale and scope of our capital investment plans in 2026 and 2027 in order to build out the revenue-enabling capacity required to support the strengthening demand we see ahead...

The investments we are making in new capacity, which we expect will come online throughout 2026 and 2027 are a response to record bookings, accelerating growth in the scale of our existing engagements and meaningfully improved long-term demand visibility with our hyperscaler customers.

- CFO Mandeep Chawla in the Q4 FY25 earnings call.

The capex spends will be routed to vastly expand manufacturing capacity in Texas, Thailand, Mexico, and Japan. Ultimately, I expect this to lead to continued high and potentially accelerating rates of revenue growth in the CCS segment:

The CCS segment is the big growth driver for the company and its revenue mix is steadily increasing to 78% as of the latest quarter:

Hence, we can expect TTM revenues to be boosted, too, as a result. I have put in about 35% YoY growth for FY26:

HPS product mix shifts can structurally lift margins

Celestica's CCS segment also makes up the majority (76%) of the company's overall EBIT mix:

And it is the higher-margin segment as well:

Notice how there's been a steady increase in the CCS segment margins as well. One of the reasons for this is a better product mix. Specifically, the HPS business, wherein Celestica acts as an Original Design Manufacturer (ODM) rather than a mere assembler commands higher margins, which I had estimated to be in the mid-high teens in one of my earlier articles on CLS:

I estimate hyperscaler HPS margins to be in the mid-high teens, since management has shared that it is below 20% in the latest earnings call.

- Hunting Alphas' past article on Celestica.

Now, HPS revenues have been the fastest-growing piece in Celestica's business:

And I only expect this to continue, especially as the company aids Alphabet's big spends in AI rollouts. Naturally, all this bodes rather well for the margin outlook ahead. I believe we can reach 10% EBIT margins within the next 18 months.

High customer concentration is still a risk to monitor

One thing that has not changed much for Celestica is the high concentration risk to a few customers. In Q4 FY25, 3 customers made up almost 63% of overall revenues. From accumulated knowledge in covering the stock over multiple quarters, my best guess is that they are Alphabet, Amazon (AMZN) and Dell (DELL). Tracking the capex spends of these key players would be a key monitorable. So far, it is working out favorably in Celestica's favor.

Valuations seem attractive for long term investors

Celestica is trading at a 1-yr fwd P/E of 35.7x, implying a 68% premium to its comps' median multiple of 21.2x:

This means CLS stock has gotten relatively cheaper than what it was near the end of last year, where it commanded a 107% premium over its peers.

Also, although the stock has seen a correction in both its valuation multiple and earnings expectations since the start of the year, I note that most of the correction is driven by a contraction in the valuation multiple:

I interpret this as a sign that the selloff in Celestica has been mostly driven by sentimental factors, rather than any meaningful deterioration in the market's fundamental expectations. This gives long-term investors an opportunity to load up on the stock.

Technicals show an A+ bull-flag continuation setup

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

CLS Technical Analysis

CLS is in an overall uptrend and there has been a re-accumulation bull-flag for the past 5 months. Now, after a couple of key rejections from a key support level, we are seeing some bullish engulfs forming, indicating strong momentum. I expect a new blast up to new highs. This is an A+ setup in my view.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bullish due to strong demand trends and margin expansion from improved product mix.
  • Valuation: Neutral due to rich valuation vs peers but signs of correction driven more by sentiment rather than fundamentals degradation.
  • Technical Analysis: Bullish due to A+ technical buy setup.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bullish, Strong Buy.

data.title: Abercrombie & Fitch: Aggressive Share Repurchases Paying Off - Mixed Recovery Prospects
data.description:
I previously covered Abercrombie & Fitch Co. (ANF) in January 2026, discussing how its overly done rally had triggered my downgraded rating to Hold, despite the still positive comparable sales growth trends and the resilient profit margins.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am upgrading ANF as a Buy here, thanks to the correction occurring as posited, aided by the accretive impact of the ongoing share repurchases on their adj EPS growth prospects.

ANF Faces Numerous Tailwinds & Headwinds

For now, ANF's prior December 2025 rally has proven to be overly done, with the stock losing much of those gains to breach my previously offered base-case fair value estimates of $90.60 in the last article before finding a floor at the $81s in March 2025.

These developments naturally lent credibility to my prior Hold rating, since the stock was unable to hold on to their prior marker rotation gains, worsened by the volatility arising from the beta coefficient of 1.36x and the elevated short interest ratio of 6.73%.

1. Uncertain Consumer Spending Trends

Much of ANF's headwinds are attributed to its retail prospects being closely linked to the consumer discretionary spending trends.

For now, the macroeconomic environment appears to be uncertain indeed, given the potentially elevated inflationary pressure in March 2026 (from the higher oil prices from the prior Iran conflict) and the ongoing divergence in the consumer spending trends in the currently K-shaped economy.

Given the potential deterioration in the consumer spending trends and ANF's consequently impacted FY2026 growth prospects after the notably decelerating FY2025 growth profile, I can understand why the stock already lost -34.9% of its value between my last article and the worst of the March 2026 selloff.

2. Impacted Performance Metrics From Mixed Audience Response

This is especially since ANF has reported a seasonally weaker performance in FQ4'25, as similarly observed in:

  • the comparable sales at +1% YoY and
  • the mixed operating margins of 14.1% (-2.1 points YoY/-3.6 from FY2019 levels of 10.5%), partly attributed to tariffs at -360 basis points,

compared to FQ4'24 levels of +14% YoY and 16.2% (+0.9 points YoY), respectively.

Much of ANF's headwinds are naturally attributed to their core brand's weaker demand, with the Abercrombie brand reporting lower comparable sales by -1% YoY in FQ4'25 and -7% YoY in FY2025, against a year ago at +5% YoY and +15% YoY, respectively.

This is despite the management's ongoing Abercrombie brand diversification strategy across different age/target groups, the numerous brand partnerships including NFL, the increased digital channel focus, and the prior diversification in operation model across wholesaling and licensing (department stores).

For now, the Abercrombie brand headwinds have been well balanced by the Hollister brand, with the latter still reporting the more than decent comparable sales growth by +3% YoY in FQ4'25 and +13% YoY in FY2025, albeit notably decelerating from a year ago at +24% YoY and +19% YoY, respectively.

Hollister has been able to notably drive higher unit and Average Unit Retail [AUR] selling trends, aided by the "great response from a variety of exciting marketing campaigns supporting key product drops" - all of which already triggering the higher customer retention and the new customer engagement trends.

These imply that Abercrombie's offerings have yet to resonate with their "core target audience of middle‑ to high‑income consumers," against Hollister's ongoing success with their "teen customer" target audience.

As discussed above, the K shaped economy, the reduced consumer spending trends, the growing secondhand fashion market, and the intensifying competition from Shein may have also contributed to Abercrombie brand's mixed sales performance thus far.

3. Reversal Prospects

For now, ANF has highlighted their plans to drive further growth opportunities through new store locations and store remodels, along with their future diversification in operation model beyond licensing/wholesaling to franchising.

This is on top of the ongoing price hikes for FY2026, with these efforts likely to be top-line accretive while supporting the management's decelerating (yet still decent) preliminary FY2026 guidance of:

  • net sales growth by +4% YoY at the midpoint,
  • operating margins of 12.25% (-1.05 points YoY), and
  • EPS of $10.60 (+1.3% YoY),

compared to the FY2025 performance at +6% YoY, 13.3% (-1.7 points YoY), and $10.46 (-2.1% YoY), respectively.

Given their ability to (still) drive positive comparable sales through the Hollister brand, it is unsurprising that the consensus forward estimates remain rather promising, with ANF expected to report a more than decent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +3.5%/+11% through FY2028.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a 4Y CAGR of +9.1%/+22.7% (attributed to the unprofitable FY2020) and a 10Y CAGR of +4.1%/+24.3%, respectively, with it underscoring their resilience despite the uncertain macroeconomic environment.

4. Headwinds Already Baked Into Valuations

For now, ANF remains well supported along the 1Y P/E mean of ~8x, as similarly observed in the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 9.07x.

Given the discount from the 5Y mean of 26.11x, the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 20.06x, and the sector median of 15.03x, there remains the potentially rich capital appreciation prospects upon the retailer's improved performance metrics and the consequent upward P/E re-rating.

The same has also been observed in ANF's estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.82x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 9.07x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +11%, compared to the sector median of 1.45x.

When comparing ANF's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 9.07x and 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.82x to its US-based Consumer Apparel Retail peers, including:

  • American Eagle Outfitters, Inc. (AEO) at 9.85x/0.50x,
  • The Gap, Inc. (GAP) at 10.75x/0.95x, and
  • Urban Outfitters, Inc. (URBN) at 11x/1.41x, respectively,

it is apparent that ANF, along with its similarly discounted peers, are still attractively valued here, despite the former's recent recovery by +55.7% from the 52 week lows.

ANF Is A Great Contrarian Buy Here

Thanks to the recent bounce off the $81s in March 2026, it appears that ANF has found a new trading floor while consolidating near to the 50/100/200 day moving averages.

I am of the opinion that the stock is likely to continue trading sideways at these levels in the near-term, since it is currently trading near to my fair value estimates of $94.80, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 9.07x (near to its peer group P/E mean of ~10x) and the FY2025 EPS performance at $10.46 (-2.1% YoY).

Otherwise, based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $13.50, there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +20% to my long-term price target of $122.40.

Thanks to the still rich free cash flows of $378.37M in FY2025 (-28.2% YoY) and the healthy balance sheet with zero debts (aside from operating lease liabilities), readers must note that ANF has been returning great value to long-term shareholders.

This has been observed in the -10.7% of its float retired in FY2025 and -27% since FY2019, as the management continues "to expect share repurchases will be the primary use of free cash flow."

With $850M still remaining on their share repurchase authorization, it is unsurprising that the consensus adj EPS growth estimates through FY2028 appear rather impressive, thanks to the accretive impact of the smaller float, as similarly observed in the FY2025 net income performance at -10.4% YoY against the EPS growth by -2.1% YoY.

As a result of the management still signaling robust, profitable growth trends in FY2026, the discounted valuations from the prior correction, and the excellent dual return prospects across capital appreciation/richer equities, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the ANF stock here.

data.title: AI Capex Surge, Bottlenecks, And The Race For ROI
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Two of Seeking Alpha's top tech analysts, Amrita Roy and Uttam Dey, share how they approach the sector (1:00) ROI on AI capex; hyperscalers, optical connectivity and memory (11:45) Lumentum bullishness and why gross margins are so important (26:00) Nvidia's peculiar spot (30:30) Tech stock mispricings (37:00) Meta's macro backdrop (47:00)

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Very happy to welcome to Investing Experts, Amrita Roy and Uttam Dey, two analysts that have been much talked about. Welcome to you both. Very, very happy to have you both on.

Amrita Roy & Uttam Dey: Thank you. Thank you very much. Very excited to be on the podcast.

Rena Sherbill: Yeah, we've had a lot of great things said about your analysis by other analysts. I think Gary Vaughan called you his favorite analyst on Seeking Alpha. I don't think I'm overstating that. I think that's what he called you so your reputations precede you.

Amrita, you have an investing group also on Seeking Alpha called the REIT Forum, is that correct?

Amrita Roy: That's correct. So I collaborate with Colorado Wealth Management on his investing group, though I do not actively contribute because most of my analysis is mostly on tech. But yes, that is correct.

Rena Sherbill: Okay, wanted to make sure about that. So maybe if you would both introduce yourselves and your strategy. Did I mention that you're married? That might be our first time having a married couple on the show, which I bet lends itself. Yeah, yeah, go ahead.

Amrita Roy: Yeah, we have been married a very long time, probably eight years, together for probably what 11 or 12 years. But yes, we work very well together. And it was always our dream to sort of do this thing where we each have our individual strengths to bring together, especially investing.

Rena Sherbill: Did investing bring you guys together? Did that have anything to do with you meeting?

Amrita Roy: That would have been cute. But no, actually we met in university and both of us have quite extensive backgrounds working in technology companies in the Bay Area. So Uttam actually worked in the larger, well-established companies like Apple and Google.

I worked in various startups that eventually some of them got sold to larger companies. So yeah, so we do come from a very deep technology background. And I think probably around like 2018, 2019, when it was RobinHood picking up steam in like the local conversations, were probably one of the early users and sort of really got into this active investing phase in our lives.

And probably around 2020, 2021, I think both of us wanted a career pivot where we just wanted to understand more about just core investing in general and just really connect the dots, understand innovation cycles, how each company sort of fit into those cycles, how to sort of understand balance sheets, understand financial statements to really understand how demand cycles and margins are about to inflect higher or lower and how that dictates price actions on various stocks.

We got deep into that and that's how our investing journey started. We started publishing on Seeking Alpha. We also have our own substack group where we publish our research. And yeah, it's been what, two years that we're doing it full-time and absolutely loving it.

Rena Sherbill: I see that that coincided with COVID. Did that macro picture lend itself to that decision?

Amrita Roy: Absolutely. Yes. I think it was probably the first time where I took an active interest in macro because I think prior to that, I didn't really pay much attention to macro at all. But yeah, it was probably the first time that I really wanted to understand and connect the dots in how sort of global financial economies work.

Uttam Dey: And then in addition to Amrita going macro, my focus would always be going towards the industry side and trying to understand which industry is moving up and down in the various demand cycles and get deep inside the industry as well, getting that subject matter expertise there.

So I think those two different kinds of research methodologies to just amalgamate and that's how this research started moving ahead towards investments.

Rena Sherbill: Well, first of all, sounds as I'm hearing you both talk, it sounds a little bit like when I started at Seeking Alpha, it was in 2008 and the great financial crisis. And I felt like that was the number one thing that I was taking away from my time at Seeking Alpha, which was connecting these dots and seeing this macro picture that felt very scary and something that felt very confusing and what the heck is going on. And if you start connecting the dots, it may not make you feel better, but it makes a lot more sense.

Amrita Roy: I know.

Rena Sherbill: And when you're talking about the tech space, you probably couldn't have, I mean, maybe you could talk about different times in history, but these past two years in the tech space are supremely interesting. And I bet it lends itself to a lot of thought provoking conversations between the two of you.

So maybe ground us how you're thinking about, maybe how you've been thinking about the past couple of years and then bring us up to date with how you're thinking about today. I'll let you decide who goes first. You guys probably have it worked out better than I do.

Uttam Dey: Investing in high growth assets, the kind that tend to generate strong alpha that generally sit in AI or the digitally native ecosystem. So that's like what the market or the investment industry just classifies as the technology sector. We just spoke about technology a lot and how that happens to be our background and it's just running in our veins. we invest in our areas of expertise.

But to the point that you just mentioned earlier, these last two years have been just crazy for technology. So of late, what we've started doing is we've started utilizing a lot of thematic investment strategies to deploy our capital, possibly even in multi-phased approaches.

Because let's face it, this is the AI era and while that's exciting, there's lots of extremely fast movements in terms of AI innovation, capital investments, R&D.

There's lots of competitive positioning also that's quickly moving and it's really rapid. In one quarter of the industry we're talking about investing in picks and shovels and the other quarter of the industry we're talking about where the memory shortages are.

Nowadays the word on everyone's lips is bottlenecks, right? So the point is, in AI, it's just fast and furious and AI is moving really fast. And with that, portfolio capital is following rapidly as well.

And so we've adjusted our portfolio strategy as well to not just be secular, but also have some kind of thematic investment to accommodate for those kinds of strategies as well.

On a more tactical basis, I would say like we tend to go top down a lot. So we look at industries on the macro level. We look at dependencies, competitive positioning, and then try to move down through the industry and pick out winners.

Winners which will, they'll deliver these category leading performances over the rest of its peers. And you would have already seen this kind of research in both our research on the platform.

When it comes to capital deployment, finally, I think that's the last bit I just wanted to say, when it comes to capital deployment, we usually just start layering into a position.

We start underweight and then as our conviction with the name starts getting a lot better, we'll build our exposure to full weight or even overweight, depending on how our conviction flows.

But then when it comes to, let's say, thematic investing, we might just go really aggressive on a particular name. But that's all based on how our risk and our portfolio management strategies fit in.

Amrita Roy: I think just to summarize, AI is not a constant. Over the last two years, we have seen leadership change shift from AI accelerators to now in the bottleneck trades, such as memory, optics, networking, and things like that.

In the meantime, we have also seen certain corners of the industry which used to generate alpha, like software, completely get disrupted, as well as cybersecurity, which is currently sort going through this really messy stage in between.

So I think a big chunk of our job really is to just understand where these bottlenecks are and where the next bottleneck could be. And at the same time, maybe a lot of the time it's also about identifying where a lot of the mispricing could be as well, where demand may be about to inflect, but the market has no idea or the market is not pricing in that the demand is about to inflect higher. And it's a constant process.

Given the pace of AI progress, which is so much faster than like, let's say cloud computing or the previous innovation waves, this is sort of like a quarterly thing that we have quarterly or even a monthly thing that we have to be constantly updating ourselves on to remain on the right side of the trade, essentially.

Rena Sherbill: What's your process like in terms of are you each interested? Do you guys each analyze the same data and then come together and discuss it. Are you each analyzing different points different stocks? How do you guys blend your processes or work together and also just curious how do you decide which articles, which stocks that you each write about?

Amrita Roy: Sure, I can go for that. I think in terms of the AI supply chain, if you look at it, I cover most things that sort of at the top of the AI supply chains are things like hyperscalers, software, cybersecurity.

Those are my sort of expert areas, whereas Uttam covers more of the picks and shovels like semiconductors, so accelerators, custom XPUs, networking optics, memory, and things like that.

Our stocks rarely, the kind of research we publish on Seeking Alpha, the stocks never collide with each other given our individual sort of expertise expert areas. But they're all part of the same AI ecosystem.

I do my own research, but at the same time, it's the same top-down research that both of us perform on our individual sectors, industries, stocks within. We obviously discuss in terms of our overall portfolio strategy, how do we go about, given sort of the risk reward we are seeing across the stocks that we have identified as potential winners. And based on that, we decide on capital allocation, price points, entry, exit strategies, et cetera.

Rena Sherbill: Okay, so in terms of thematic investing, what would you say are the top two themes that you're thinking about and what stocks are doing in those subsectors accordingly?

Amrita Roy: Again, I think I will start at a high level because I think and then you can sort of and I will point out one core theme within and then you can talk about your themes in the stocks.
